summary: Gruber reports on Signal's new Secure Backup feature finally arriving on iOS, which allows end-to-end encrypted cloud backups of chat history. He shares his own experience where the old device-to-device transfer crashed and lost all his data on both phones, but the new cloud backup restored everything perfectly in about one minute. He argues that Signal's long-standing philosophy of keeping data only on-device was a form of insecurity in itself, since losing your phone meant losing everything. While acknowledging Signal's noble security priorities, he contends the lack of cloud backup for years was user-hostile and held Signal back from mainstream adoption.
key_points: Signal Secure Backups offer free encrypted cloud backup of chat text plus 45 days of attachments, or $2/month for full 100GB media backup
Gruber's device-to-device Signal transfer crashed at 100% completion, wiping his account history on both phones — proving the old system's fragility
The cloud backup restored everything in about one minute versus the 15 minutes the failed device-to-device transfer took
Permanently losing all your data is a different form of insecurity than having it exfiltrated — Signal prioritized one threat while ignoring the other
The feature should have existed years ago; its absence kept Signal from achieving mainstream adoption
money_quotes: Transferring my Signal account history from one phone to another always felt like delivering a crate full of eggs while riding a rickety old bicycle without brakes on a bumpy cobblestone street.
There's nothing "secure" about a transfer feature that loses all of your data if the transfer fails.

Permanently losing all your data is a different form of "insecurity" than having it exfiltrated by an attacker or exposed to law enforcement agencies via a warrant issued to the cloud backup provider, but it's a form of insecurity nonetheless.

Signal's top priority has always been protecting your data from being obtained by others. That's a noble idea, and central to Signal's brand. But by placing that priority so far above everything else, it meant, until now, that you'd lose your entire account history if you lost or broke your primary phone.

A user-hostile "lose your phone, lose your account history" architecture may well be "secure" in a technical sense, but it's the sort of brittleness that's kept Signal from achieving more mainstream use.
I'm glad this feature became available when it did, and that I enabled it over the weekend.
Now that I'm paying $2/month, however, I wish they'd stop periodically badgering me for donations when I launch the app.
key_insight: True security must protect users from data loss as well as data theft — Signal's new cloud backup finally addresses the user-hostile brittleness that kept it from mainstream adoption.
